1:
On Deuteronomy 5:1 — “And Moses called all Israel, and said unto them, Hear, O Israel, the statutes and judgments which I speak in your ears this day, that ye may learn them, and keep, and do them” — the witnesses mark Moses summoning all Israel to hear the law.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: the exposition of the law begins with a repetition of the ten words of the covenant, spoken to all Israel directly by the Lord. Charles Ellicott: what follows is an actual exhortation, not merely a portion of a book; the statutes and judgments, the religious ordinances and requirements. The Puritan Henry: Moses called all Israel, not the elders only but as many as could hear; the greatest were not above God’s command nor the meanest beneath it.

The verse opens the discourse. Moses calls all Israel to hear, learn, keep, and do the statutes. The whole people are summoned to the law.

So the witnesses see Moses gather the whole congregation to hear afresh the law of God, that they might learn and do it. The witnesses note the breadth of the summons: “Moses called all Israel” — Henry observes that he called not the elders only but as many of the people as could come within hearing; for the greatest of them were not above God’s command, nor the meanest beneath its reach; the whole nation stood under the law alike. They mark the place of this discourse, which Keil and Delitzsch note: here begins Moses’ exposition of the law, opening with a repetition of the ten words of the covenant which God had spoken directly to all Israel. They observe Ellicott’s point that what follows is presented as a living exhortation, not a mere written record; Moses speaks the statutes “in your ears this day,” pressing them on the present generation. They mark the fourfold charge: “that ye may learn them, and keep, and do them” — the law was to be heard, learned, kept in memory and heart, and done in life; hearing was to issue in obedience. They note the lesson the witnesses draw: that the word of God is given not for knowledge only but for practice; to learn without doing is to fail of its end. They observe that Moses, near the close of his life, would have the people fix the law in their hearts before they entered the land. They note that all are called to hear and obey. The verse thus opens the discourse: all Israel summoned to learn and do — the covenant of Horeb recalled in the next verses.
2:
On Deuteronomy 5:2 — “The LORD our God made a covenant with us in Horeb” — the witnesses mark the covenant of Horeb.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: Jehovah our God made a covenant with us in Horeb. Charles Ellicott: it must never be forgotten that the Law is a covenant in its very form. The Reformed Gill: Horeb, the same as Sinai, the same mountain with two tops bearing different names. Calvin: he commends the Law; it is a peculiar blessing and a high honour to be taken into covenant by God.

The verse names the covenant. The LORD made a covenant with Israel at Horeb. The law is set forth as a covenant.

So the witnesses see Moses recall the covenant God made with Israel at Horeb, the very form and ground of the law. The witnesses note the place: “in Horeb” — Gill observes that Horeb is the same as Sinai, the same mountain having two tops or peaks that bore these different names; there the decalogue was given. They mark the great truth Ellicott presses, that must never be forgotten: the Law is in its very form a covenant — not a bare code of rules, but the terms of a relationship between God and his people, sealed by mutual engagement. They observe Calvin’s commendation of the Law that flows from this: that it must be accounted a peculiar blessing and a very high honour to be taken into covenant by God; the giving of the law was an act of grace, drawing Israel near to God as his own; that they might prepare themselves to embrace it, Moses sets it forth as a covenant-privilege. They note the dignity the witnesses see in Israel’s standing: that the living God condescended to bind himself to a people, and to bind them to himself, by a covenant; the law was the charter of that bond. They observe that the covenant of Horeb was the foundation of all that followed in the nation’s life. They note that to be in covenant with God is the highest of mercies. The verse thus names the covenant of Horeb — its parties pressed home in the next verse.
3:
On Deuteronomy 5:3 — “The LORD made not this covenant with our fathers, but with us, even us, who are all of us here alive this day” — the witnesses mark the covenant pressed on the living generation.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: “not with our fathers only, but with us also.” Charles Ellicott: by the usage of Hebrew in drawing contrasts, not only with the fathers who heard it, but with us also, who were in their loins. The Reformed Gill: not with the fathers only, as Jarchi and Aben Ezra remark. Wesley: not that all who made the covenant at Sinai are now alive, but this covenant was made with all now alive.

The verse presses the covenant home. Not with the fathers only, but with the living generation. Each age stands in the covenant as its own.

So the witnesses see Moses press the covenant of Horeb upon the present generation as truly their own. The witnesses note the apparent denial and its true sense: “the LORD made not this covenant with our fathers, but with us” — the words seem to exclude the fathers, but the witnesses agree (Clarke, Ellicott, Gill) that the Hebrew idiom of drawing a contrast means “not with our fathers only, but with us also”; the covenant was not a dead thing belonging to a past generation, but a living bond binding those then alive. They mark Ellicott’s point that the present generation were in the loins of their fathers when the covenant was made, and so were party to it; and Wesley’s careful balance, that not all who stood at Sinai were now alive (most had died in the wilderness), yet the covenant was made with all who were now alive, having been made with the nation in its continuing life. They observe the force Moses gives it: he will not let the people treat the law as their fathers’ affair, a matter of old history; he brings it home to them, “even us, who are all of us here alive this day,” that each generation might own the covenant as personally binding. They note the abiding lesson the witnesses draw: that God’s covenant and word are not to be put off as belonging to former times, but are addressed to every living soul that hears them; the law speaks to us as truly as to the fathers. They observe that the covenant renews its claim on each age. The verse thus presses the covenant on the living — the face-to-face speaking recalled in the next verse.
4:
On Deuteronomy 5:4 — “The LORD talked with you face to face in the mount out of the midst of the fire” — the witnesses mark God speaking face to face.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: yet they saw no similitude (Deuteronomy 4:12), no visible form, but the very words reached their ears. The Reformed Gill: not in the familiar manner as with Moses, but publicly, audibly, clearly. Calvin: he commends the Law by their certainty of it; when God openly manifested himself, there could be no doubt of its author. Wesley: face to face, personally and immediately, not by the ministry of Moses; plainly and certainly.

The verse marks the directness. God talked with Israel face to face from the fire. The law came immediately from God.

So the witnesses see Moses recall how God spoke the law to Israel directly, face to face, from the fire. The witnesses note the directness: “the LORD talked with you face to face” — Gill and Wesley explain this not of the familiar, friendly manner in which God spoke with Moses (Exodus 33:11), but of God’s speaking the ten words publicly, audibly, and immediately to the whole people, not by the mouth or ministry of any man; the words reached every ear directly from God. They mark the careful qualification Ellicott adds: though God spoke “face to face,” yet the people saw no manner of similitude, no visible form (Deuteronomy 4:12); they heard the very words of God, but beheld no shape; the “face to face” is of the voice, not of sight. They observe the use Calvin and Wesley make of this directness: that it gave the people full certainty of the law’s divine author; when God thus openly manifested himself and spoke, there could be no doubt from whom the law proceeded; the people had it not at second hand but from God’s own mouth. They note the awe of it: that the living God descended in fire and spoke to a whole nation, a thing never done before or since; the law came with the highest possible authority. They observe that this directness both honoured the law and prepared the terror that would follow. They note that God’s word to his people is sure, having come from himself. The verse thus marks the face-to-face speaking — Moses’ mediation noted in the next verse.
5:
On Deuteronomy 5:5 — “I stood between the LORD and you at that time, to shew you the word of the LORD: for ye were afraid by reason of the fire, and went not up into the mount” — the witnesses mark Moses the mediator.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: I stood between the LORD and you, to shew you the word of the LORD, for ye were afraid by reason of the fire. Charles Ellicott: read “I stood between Jehovah and you (for ye were afraid), and ye went not up into the mount.” The Reformed Gill: between the Word of the Lord and you, as the Targums. Wesley: as a mediator, according to your desire; “the word of the Lord,” not the ten commandments, which God uttered, but the following statutes.

The verse names Moses mediator. He stood between the LORD and the people, for they feared the fire. The mediator declares God’s word.

So the witnesses see Moses set forth as the mediator who stood between God and the fearful people. The witnesses note his office: “I stood between the LORD and you at that time, to shew you the word of the LORD” — Wesley explains that Moses acted as a mediator, at the people’s own desire, conveying God’s word to them; Gill notes the Targums read “between the Word of the Lord and you,” Moses standing as the appointed go-between. They mark the reason of his mediation: “for ye were afraid by reason of the fire, and went not up into the mount” — the people, terrified by the fire and the voice, dared not draw near nor go up, and so needed one to stand between them and God and bring them his word. They observe the distinction Wesley draws as to what Moses mediated: not the ten commandments themselves, which God spoke directly to all the people with his own voice, but the further statutes and judgments, which God gave through Moses; the Decalogue came from God immediately, the rest through the mediator. They note the typology the witnesses see, though chiefly drawn out elsewhere: that Moses standing between the holy God and the trembling people, declaring God’s word to those who could not bear his immediate presence, foreshadows the true Mediator, Christ, who stands between God and men (1 Timothy 2:5; Galatians 3:19). They observe that the people’s fear made a mediator needful, as sinners’ fear of God ever does. They note that God provided a go-between in mercy. The verse thus names Moses the mediator — the Decalogue’s preface following in the next verse.
6:
On Deuteronomy 5:6 — “I am the LORD thy God, which brought thee out of the land of Egypt, from the house of bondage” — the witnesses mark the preface of the commandments, redemption the ground of the law.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: this sentence is an integral part of the Decalogue, and its first part — the declaration of divine relationship, the covenanted adoption of Israel by God. Adam Clarke refers to the commandments explained at Exodus 20:1. The Puritan Henry: though spoken and written before, they are again rehearsed; precept upon precept. The Reformed Gill: the same preface as in Exodus, the commands in the same order with little variation.

The verse gives the preface. The LORD who redeemed them from Egypt is their God. Redemption is the ground of obedience.

So the witnesses see the Decalogue opened, as in Exodus, with the great preface that grounds all the commandments in God’s redeeming grace. The witnesses note the weight Ellicott lays on these words: “I am the LORD thy God” is not a mere introduction but an integral part of the Decalogue, indeed its first part — the declaration of the divine relationship, the covenanted adoption of Israel by God as his own people; the law rests on this bond. They mark the ground of obedience: “which brought thee out of the land of Egypt, from the house of bondage” — God claims their obedience as the God who had redeemed them from slavery; because he had freed them, they owed him their whole allegiance; grace is the spring of duty, redemption the reason of the law. They observe Henry’s note on the repetition of the commandments here: though they had been spoken and written before, they are rehearsed again, for precept must be upon precept and line upon line, and all little enough to keep the people mindful; the law bears repeating. They observe Gill’s and Clarke’s reminder that the commands stand here in the same order and nearly the same words as in Exodus 20:1, with a few variations to be marked as they come. They note the gospel pattern the witnesses see: that God first redeems and then commands; the law is given to a people already saved, that they might live as his. They observe that obedience flows from gratitude for redemption. The verse thus gives the preface — the first commandment following in the next verse.
7:
On Deuteronomy 5:7 — “Thou shalt have none other gods before me” — the witnesses mark the first commandment.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: thou shalt have none other gods before me. Charles Ellicott: literally, “upon my face,” in addition to my presence; in any place where I am, that is, in the whole world (Psalm 139:7). The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley adds searching questions: hast thou worshipped no god but the God of Israel? Is he the God of thy life, thy hope, thy love?

The verse gives the first command. No other gods before the LORD. He alone is to be worshipped.

So the witnesses see the first commandment, forbidding all other gods, the foundation of the whole law. The witnesses note the force of the phrase: “none other gods before me” — Ellicott explains the Hebrew as “upon my face,” that is, in addition to my presence, before my very eyes; and since God is everywhere, in any place where he is — that is, in the whole world — no other god may be set up; there is no spot where his presence is not, and so no place where a rival may be admitted (Psalm 139:7). They mark the sweep of the command: it claims for the LORD the sole place in his people’s worship and trust; to have any other god, in heart or in act, is to break it. They observe the inward, searching application that Wesley presses, drawing the commandment home to the conscience: hast thou worshipped no god but the God of Israel? Is he the God of thy life, the strength of thy heart, the object of thy supreme love and trust? Wesley turns each command into a question for the soul, that the reader may answer between God and himself; the first commandment asks whether God has the whole heart. They note the heart of the matter the witnesses see: that the first commandment is not only against the idols of the heathen but against every rival in the affections — whatever a man loves, fears, or trusts above God is another god before him. They observe that to keep this command is to give God his sole and rightful place. They note that all the rest depend on this. The verse thus gives the first commandment — the second following in the next verse.
8:
On Deuteronomy 5:8 — “Thou shalt not make thee any graven image, or any likeness of any thing… in heaven… in the earth… in the waters” — the witnesses mark the second commandment.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley presses the inward sense: hast thou not formed any gross image of God in thy mind? Hast thou always thought of him as a pure spirit, whom no man hath seen nor can see? And hast thou worshipped him in spirit and in truth?

The verse gives the second command. No graven image or likeness for worship. God is not to be imaged.

So the witnesses see the second commandment, forbidding the making and worshipping of any image of God. The witnesses note the breadth of the prohibition: “any graven image, or any likeness of any thing that is in heaven… in the earth… in the waters” — nothing in the whole creation, above or below, was to be fashioned into an image of God or made an object of worship; the command sweeps away every visible representation of the invisible God. They mark the ground the witnesses see in it (drawn out more fully at Deuteronomy 4:15): that the people saw no manner of similitude when God spoke from the fire, and so were not to imagine that he could be set forth by any image; to image God is to debase him, to mistake the Creator for the creature. They observe the inward and spiritual application Wesley presses: that the command reaches not only to graven idols of wood and stone, but to the images men form of God in their minds; hast thou not framed any gross or unworthy conception of God in thy thoughts, but always thought of him as a pure spirit, whom no eye hath seen? — and hast thou worshipped him in spirit and in truth, with the heart and not the eye? They note the lesson the witnesses draw: that God will be worshipped as he is, a spirit, and not under any form of man’s devising; the second commandment guards the spirituality of his worship. They observe that to image God is to break it, whether in metal or in mind. The verse thus gives the second commandment — its sanction following in the next verses.
9:
On Deuteronomy 5:9 — “Thou shalt not bow down thyself unto them, nor serve them: for I the LORD thy God am a jealous God, visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the children” — the witnesses mark the jealous God and the visiting of iniquity.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: there are no sins which so surely entail penal consequences on succeeding generations as the abominations of idolatry; all idolatry degrades the divine image. The Reformed Calvin: idolaters vainly elude this by their cavils, as the Papists’ trifling distinction of latria and dulia.

The verse gives the sanction. God is jealous, visiting the fathers’ idolatry on the children. The command is guarded by his jealousy.

So the witnesses see the second commandment guarded by the declaration of God’s jealousy and his visiting of idolatry upon generations. The witnesses note the prohibition of worship: “thou shalt not bow down thyself unto them, nor serve them” — not the making only, but the bowing and serving of images is forbidden; the command reaches to every act of religious homage paid to them. They mark the ground in God’s nature: “for I the LORD thy God am a jealous God” — God is jealous for his own honour and his people’s undivided worship, and will not give his glory to another; idolatry provokes his jealousy as a wife’s unfaithfulness provokes her husband. They observe Calvin’s sharp word against the evasions of idolaters: that they vainly try to elude this command by their cavils, as the Papists with their trifling distinction between latria (worship due to God) and dulia (a lesser veneration given to images and saints); God forbids all bowing and serving of images, and no subtle distinction escapes the command. They note the dread sanction: “visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the children” — Ellicott observes that no sins so surely entail penal consequences on after-generations as the abominations of idolatry, which degrade the very image of God in man and pass their corruption down the line; the children who follow the fathers’ idolatry share the fathers’ doom. They note that the jealousy of God is the guard of true worship. They observe that idolatry is no light or private sin, but one whose curse reaches far. The verse thus gives the sanction — the mercy to the faithful following in the next verse.
10:
On Deuteronomy 5:10 — “And shewing mercy unto thousands of them that love me and keep my commandments” — the witnesses mark the mercy to those that love God.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: an echo of this in our Saviour’s words, “If ye love me, keep my commandments” (John 14:15); the promise of his presence by the Comforter.

The verse gives the mercy. God shews mercy to thousands that love him and keep his commands. Grace outruns the threat.

So the witnesses see the threatening of the second commandment balanced by a far larger promise of mercy to those who love God. The witnesses note the breadth of the grace: “shewing mercy unto thousands of them that love me and keep my commandments” — over against the visiting of iniquity to the third and fourth generation stands mercy to thousands (of generations, or of persons); the promise of grace far outruns the threat of judgment, God being more ready to bless the faithful than to punish the rebellious. They mark the marks of those to whom mercy is shown: “them that love me and keep my commandments” — love and obedience joined; the keeping of the commandments is the fruit and proof of love to God. They observe the echo Ellicott hears of this in the words of Christ: “If ye love me, keep my commandments” (John 14:15); the same union of love and obedience runs from the law into the gospel, and to those who so love and obey, Christ promises the presence of the Comforter, the Spirit, to abide with them. They note the lesson the witnesses draw: that God’s mercy is for those whose obedience springs from love, not slavish fear; the heart that loves God will keep his word, and to such the covenant mercy reaches down the generations. They observe that the gospel does not abolish but fulfils this bond of love and obedience. They note that grace abounds over judgment. The verse thus gives the mercy to those that love God — the third commandment following in the next verse.
11:
On Deuteronomy 5:11 — “Thou shalt not take the name of the LORD thy God in vain; for the LORD will not hold him guiltless that taketh his name in vain” — the witnesses mark the third commandment.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: literally, “thou shalt not put the name of Jehovah to vanity,” to anything false, hollow, or unreal; primarily false swearing, but the principle extends further. The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley presses the conscience: hast thou never used the name of God but on weighty occasions, and then with the deepest awe? Hast thou honoured his word, his ordinances, his name?

The verse gives the third command. The name of God is not to be taken in vain. His name is to be held holy.

So the witnesses see the third commandment, forbidding the vain or empty use of God’s holy name. The witnesses note the sense of the words: “take the name of the LORD thy God in vain” — Ellicott renders it literally “thou shalt not put the name of Jehovah to vanity,” that is, to anything that is false, hollow, or unreal; the name of God is not to be linked to falsehood or emptiness. They mark the primary reference Ellicott gives: false swearing is chiefly forbidden — the calling of God to witness a lie, or the breaking of an oath taken in his name; but the principle extends much further, to every light, profane, or empty use of the holy name. They observe the searching application Wesley presses on the conscience: hast thou never used the name of God except on solemn and weighty occasions, and then with the deepest awe? Hast thou duly honoured his word, his ordinances, his name? — the command reaches to all irreverent speech, careless oaths, and the profaning of holy things. They note the solemn sanction: “the LORD will not hold him guiltless that taketh his name in vain” — though men make light of profane speech, God will surely call it to account; the guilt of dishonouring his name shall not go unpunished. They observe the reverence the witnesses urge: that the name of God, in which all his glory is summed, is to be used always with awe and never in vanity. They note that to hallow God’s name is the prayer and duty of his people (Matthew 6:9). The verse thus gives the third commandment — the fourth, of the Sabbath, following in the next verses.
12:
On Deuteronomy 5:12 — “Keep the sabbath day to sanctify it, as the LORD thy God hath commanded thee” — the witnesses mark the fourth commandment, with “keep” for “remember.”

The grammatical-historical and Reformed Gill: “keep the sabbath day to sanctify it” — observe it, setting it apart for rest and holy exercises; in Exodus the phrase is “remember the sabbath day to keep it” (Exodus 20:8). The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley presses the conscience: dost thou do no work on this day which could be done as well on another? Art thou careful to avoid all that does not tend to the knowledge and love of God?

The verse gives the fourth command. The Sabbath is to be kept and sanctified. The day of rest is hallowed to God.

So the witnesses see the fourth commandment, the keeping of the Sabbath, set down here with a slight change of word from the Exodus form. The witnesses note the variation Gill marks: here it is “keep the sabbath day to sanctify it,” while in Exodus 20:8 it is “remember the sabbath day to keep it”; the two forms are of one substance — the people were both to remember the day and to keep it holy — the one word calling them to bear it in mind, the other to observe it in deed. They mark the added clause, “as the LORD thy God hath commanded thee” — pointing back to the command already given (at Sinai and in the wilderness with the manna), the Sabbath being no new thing but a standing ordinance. They observe the substance of the command: the day was to be kept and sanctified, set apart from common labour for rest and for holy exercises, hallowed to God. They observe the searching application Wesley presses: dost thou do no work on this day which might as well be done on another? Art thou careful to avoid all converse and business that does not tend to the knowledge and love of God? — the command reaching to the manner of the heart’s keeping, not the bare ceasing from toil. They note the honour put on the day: that God claims one day in seven as his own, to be kept holy by his people. They observe that the reason given here, in the verses following, differs from that in Exodus. The verse thus gives the fourth commandment — the rest of household and beast following, and the reason of redemption in the next verses.
14:
On Deuteronomy 5:14 — “But the seventh day is the sabbath of the LORD thy God… that thy manservant and thy maidservant may rest as well as thou” — the witnesses mark the Sabbath rest for servants and beasts.

The Reformed Gill: in Exodus it is more generally expressed; here the ox and ass are particularly named, that they too might rest, and the menservants and maidservants with them.

The verse extends the rest. The seventh day is rest for all — servants, ox, and ass. The Sabbath rest reaches the whole household.

So the witnesses see the Sabbath rest extended, in this Deuteronomy form, with particular care, to the servants and the beasts of the household. The witnesses note the breadth of the rest: “that thy manservant and thy maidservant may rest as well as thou” — the seventh day was a rest not for the master only, but for all under his roof and in his charge; the bondservant, the maidservant, the stranger within the gates, and even the labouring beasts were all to share the rest. They mark Gill’s observation that the Exodus form speaks more generally, while here the ox and the ass are particularly named, that they too might rest from their toil, and the menservants and maidservants with them; the Deuteronomy version draws out the mercy of the command toward the lowly and the dumb. They observe the tenderness the witnesses see in this: that God’s law cared for the rest of the servant and the beast, that none in Israel, however mean, should be worked without respite; the Sabbath was a weekly mercy to the whole household. They note the fitness of this emphasis in Deuteronomy, where (the next verse shows) the reason given is Israel’s own former bondage; a people who had been slaves were to remember their servants’ need of rest. They observe that the Sabbath was a humane as well as a holy ordinance, binding the master to give his servants their due ease. They note that God’s rest was for all his creatures. The verse thus extends the rest to servants and beasts — the reason of redemption following in the next verse.
15:
On Deuteronomy 5:15 — “And remember that thou wast a servant in the land of Egypt… therefore the LORD thy God commanded thee to keep the sabbath day” — the witnesses mark redemption as the Deuteronomy reason for the Sabbath.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: here Moses adds another reason why one day in seven should be sanctified — that the servants might rest, urged on the ground of Israel’s own former servitude. The Reformed Gill: remember thou wast a bondservant in Egypt, the house of bondage, that their hearts might be moved to pity their servants.

The verse gives the reason. Israel was a servant in Egypt, redeemed by God; therefore keep the Sabbath. Redemption grounds the rest.

So the witnesses see the Sabbath here grounded, not in the creation as in Exodus, but in Israel’s redemption from the bondage of Egypt. The witnesses note the new reason given: “remember that thou wast a servant in the land of Egypt, and that the LORD thy God brought thee out… therefore the LORD thy God commanded thee to keep the sabbath day” — whereas in Exodus 20:11 the reason of the Sabbath is that God rested on the seventh day from the work of creation, here Moses presses another and added reason: that Israel, having themselves been slaves in Egypt and there denied all rest, and now redeemed by God’s mighty hand, should the more readily grant rest to their own servants, and keep the day in thankful remembrance of their deliverance. They mark Clarke’s and Gill’s point that this reason bears especially on the mercy of the command toward servants: a people who had known the misery of bondage, and the joy of redemption, were bound to give their bondservants the weekly rest they themselves had once been denied. They observe that the two reasons — creation and redemption — are not at odds but complete each other: the Sabbath commemorates both God’s finishing of his works and his freeing of his people; rest from labour and rest from bondage meet in the day. They note the gospel pattern the witnesses see: that the people of God keep their rest in remembrance of their redemption, as the Christian rest looks to the finished work of Christ. They observe that mercy and remembrance are woven into the command. The verse thus grounds the Sabbath in redemption — the fifth commandment following in the next verse.
16:
On Deuteronomy 5:16 — “Honour thy father and thy mother, as the LORD thy God hath commanded thee; that thy days may be prolonged, and that it may go well with thee” — the witnesses mark the fifth commandment, the first with promise.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: “that it may go well with thee” — so St. Paul cites the commandment (Ephesians 6:2). The Reformed Gill: the first commandment with promise, of long life and happiness in the land of Canaan. The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley presses the conscience: hast thou not been irreverent or undutiful? Hast thou obeyed their lawful commands, loved and honoured their persons, supplied their wants?

The verse gives the fifth command. Honour father and mother, with promise of long life and well-being. Duty to parents is crowned with blessing.

So the witnesses see the fifth commandment, the honouring of parents, set down here with an added clause of promise. The witnesses note the duty: “honour thy father and thy mother” — the command to revere, obey, love, and care for those who gave us life, the first of the commandments that concern our duty to man. They mark the promise attached: “that thy days may be prolonged, and that it may go well with thee” — Gill observes this is the first commandment with promise, as the apostle Paul notes (Ephesians 6:2); the promise of long life and happiness in the land was annexed to the keeping of it; Ellicott marks that the added words “that it may go well with thee” are found here and are cited by Paul. They observe the searching application Wesley presses: hast thou not been irreverent or undutiful to either parent? Hast thou slighted their advice, or cheerfully obeyed all their lawful commands? Hast thou loved and honoured their persons, and supplied their wants in their need? — the command reaching to reverence, obedience, affection, and support. They note the fitness of the promise the witnesses see: that God should crown the honouring of parents with long life and well-being, for the duty preserves the order of families and of society, on which the welfare of a people rests. They observe that the command, with its promise, binds children in every age. They note that to honour parents is to honour God who set them over us. The verse thus gives the fifth commandment — the sixth following in the next verse.
17:
On Deuteronomy 5:17 — “Thou shalt not kill” — the witnesses mark the sixth commandment.

The grammatical-historical and Reformed Gill: the following commands begin with the copulative “and,” joining them together and to the preceding ones. The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley presses the conscience: hast thou not tempted any to what might shorten his life? Tempted none to intemperance? Done all thou couldst to preserve the life of all?

The verse gives the sixth command. Thou shalt not kill. Human life is sacred and not to be taken.

So the witnesses see the sixth commandment, forbidding the taking of human life. The witnesses note the plainness of the command: “thou shalt not kill” — the unlawful taking of a man’s life, murder, is forbidden; the command guards the sacredness of human life, which God made in his own image. They mark Gill’s small observation on the form: that here in Deuteronomy the latter commandments are joined by the copulative “and” (“and thou shalt not…”), binding them one to another and to the foregoing, that the whole law be seen as one. They observe the inward and far-reaching application Wesley presses: that the command forbids not only the act of murder but all that tends to it — hast thou not tempted any to what might shorten his life? hast thou tempted none to intemperance, nor suffered any to be intemperate under thy roof? hast thou done all thou couldst to preserve the life and health of all? — the command reaching to anger, hatred, and every neglect of a neighbour’s life, as our Lord taught (Matthew 5:21). They note the breadth the witnesses give it: that it binds us not only to do no murder, but to do all we can to preserve life, our own and our neighbour’s; the command is positive as well as negative. They observe that human life, being God’s gift and image, is to be reverenced and guarded. They note that hatred is the root the command would pluck up. The verse thus gives the sixth commandment — the seventh following in the next verse.
18:
On Deuteronomy 5:18 — “Neither shalt thou commit adultery” — the witnesses mark the seventh commandment.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley presses the conscience: if thou hast not been guilty of any act of uncleanness, hath thy heart conceived no unclean thought? Hast thou not looked on a woman so as to lust after her, betraying thy own soul?

The verse gives the seventh command. Thou shalt not commit adultery. The marriage bond and purity are guarded.

So the witnesses see the seventh commandment, forbidding adultery and guarding the purity of the marriage bond. The witnesses note the command: “neither shalt thou commit adultery” — the violation of the marriage covenant, and (by extension) all unchastity, is forbidden; the command guards the sanctity of marriage, the purity of the person, and the order of the family. They mark the inward and searching application Wesley presses, after the manner of our Lord: that the command reaches not to the outward act only, but to the heart and the eye — if thou hast not been guilty of any act of uncleanness, yet hath thy heart conceived no unclean thought? hast thou not looked upon a woman so as to lust after her, and so betrayed thine own soul? — for Christ taught that whoso looketh on a woman to lust after her hath committed adultery already in his heart (Matthew 5:28). They observe the depth the witnesses give the command: that it binds the thoughts and desires as well as the deeds, requiring purity of heart and eye, not merely abstinence from the act; the unclean thought is itself a breach. They note the honour the command puts on marriage and on chastity: that God guards the marriage bond as a sacred thing, and would have his people clean in body and soul. They observe that the seventh commandment, like the sixth, is spiritual, reaching the inward man. They note that purity is the will of God for his people (1 Thessalonians 4:3). The verse thus gives the seventh commandment — the eighth following in the next verse.

So the witnesses see, in the seventh commandment, God’s guard upon the marriage bond and the purity of his people. They observe that the command, though briefly spoken, reaches far: it forbids not the gross act of adultery only, but every breach of chastity in deed, and, as our Lord taught, in heart and eye as well. They mark that Wesley would have the reader search himself by it — not only whether his hands are clean, but whether his thoughts are; for the unclean look and the cherished lust are themselves a breaking of the command in the sight of God, who reads the heart. They note the honour the commandment puts upon marriage, that God hedges it about as a sacred and inviolable thing, the foundation of the family and of the ordered life of a people; to defile it is to sin against God who instituted it, against the wronged partner, and against one’s own soul. They observe that the keeping of this command requires not mere outward restraint but inward purity, a heart kept clean by grace; and that such purity of body and spirit is the very will of God for his people, who are called to holiness and not to uncleanness.
19:
On Deuteronomy 5:19 — “Neither shalt thou steal” — the witnesses mark the eighth commandment.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley presses the conscience: hast thou considered that the houses, lands, money, and goods thou callest thine own are not thine own, but belong to another, even God, who is the sole proprietor?

The verse gives the eighth command. Thou shalt not steal. The property of others is to be respected.

So the witnesses see the eighth commandment, forbidding theft and guarding the property of our neighbour. The witnesses note the command: “neither shalt thou steal” — the taking of what is another’s, by force, fraud, or any wrong, is forbidden; the command guards every man’s right to his own, and binds us to honesty in all our dealings. They mark the searching and humbling application Wesley presses, which goes to the root: hast thou considered that the very houses, lands, money, and goods which thou art used to call thine own are not in truth thine own, but belong to another, even God, who is the sole proprietor of all? — so that we are but stewards of what we hold, and to misuse or hoard it, or to withhold from God and the needy their due, is itself a kind of theft. They observe the breadth the witnesses give the command: that it forbids not only the open robbery of the thief, but all dishonesty, overreaching, withholding of wages, and unfaithful stewardship; and binds us positively to deal justly, to render to all their dues, and to use what we have as God’s trust. They note the lesson Wesley draws: that since all we have is God’s, the command reaches to the whole use of our substance; we steal from God when we waste or misemploy his gifts. They observe that honesty and faithful stewardship are required of God’s people. They note that the command guards the bonds of trust on which society rests. The verse thus gives the eighth commandment — the ninth following in the next verse.

So the witnesses see, in the eighth commandment, God’s guard upon the goods and rights of our neighbour, and his claim upon the use of all we hold. They observe that the command forbids every kind of taking what is not our own — not the thief’s open robbery only, but all fraud, overreaching, the withholding of wages, false dealing, and unfaithful stewardship; and that it binds us positively to honesty, to render to all their dues, and to deal justly in every transaction. They mark Wesley’s searching reminder that even what we call our own is not in truth ours but God’s, who is the sole proprietor of all; so that we are but stewards of his gifts, and we rob him when we waste, hoard, or misemploy what he has lent us, or withhold from the needy and from his service their due. They note the breadth the witnesses give the command: that it reaches to the whole use of our substance and our calling, requiring faithfulness in little and in much. They observe that honest dealing and faithful stewardship preserve the bonds of trust on which all society and all neighbourly life rest, and that to keep this command is to love our neighbour as ourselves.
20:
On Deuteronomy 5:20 — “Neither shalt thou bear false witness against thy neighbour” — the witnesses mark the ninth commandment.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley presses the conscience: hast thou not been guilty of evil-speaking, of needlessly repeating the real fault of thy neighbour? If thou see a man do an evil thing, and tell it to another, unless from real necessity, thou breakest this command.

The verse gives the ninth command. Thou shalt not bear false witness. Truth toward our neighbour is required.

So the witnesses see the ninth commandment, forbidding false witness and guarding the truth and the good name of our neighbour. The witnesses note the command: “neither shalt thou bear false witness against thy neighbour” — the giving of false testimony, especially in judgment, is forbidden; the command guards a man’s good name and the truth between man and man, on which justice and trust depend. They mark the searching breadth Wesley presses, reaching far beyond the witness-box: hast thou not been guilty of evil-speaking, of needlessly repeating the real fault of thy neighbour? for if thou see a man do an evil thing, and tell it to another, unless from real necessity or to do good, thou breakest this command — the command forbidding not only the lie sworn in court, but all slander, backbiting, tale-bearing, and the needless spreading of a neighbour’s faults. They observe the depth the witnesses give it: that it binds us to truth and to charity in all our speech of others; not only to speak no falsehood against our neighbour, but to guard his good name as our own, and to be silent of his faults where love and necessity do not require their telling. They note the lesson Wesley draws: that much of common talk, the careless repeating of others’ failings, falls under this command; the tongue must be ruled by truth and love. They observe that the command guards the bond of trust and the honour of every man. They note that to bear false witness is to wound where God commands us to love. The verse thus gives the ninth commandment — the tenth following in the next verse.
21:
On Deuteronomy 5:21 — “Neither shalt thou desire thy neighbour’s wife, neither shalt thou covet thy neighbour’s house, his field…” — the witnesses mark the tenth commandment, with the wife first and the field added.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: “his field” is not in the tenth commandment as it stands in Exodus 20:17. Charles Ellicott: these words are not in Exodus; Israel had now become, or were just to become, landowners, so the addition is appropriate; and the order is varied, the wife placed first. The Reformed Gill: here the wife is put before the house, different from Exodus. The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley: thou shalt not desire anything not thine own; why shouldst thou, since God hath given thee whatever tends to thy good?

The verse gives the tenth command. Thou shalt not covet wife, house, or field. The desire of the heart is forbidden.

So the witnesses see the tenth commandment, forbidding covetousness, set down here with some variation from the Exodus form. The witnesses note the command’s reach into the heart: “neither shalt thou desire… neither shalt thou covet” — it forbids not the act only, but the inward desire of what is another’s; the command goes deepest of all, to the heart’s wish, and so convicts of sin where no outward deed appears (Romans 7:7). They mark the variations the witnesses observe between this form and Exodus: Gill notes that here the neighbour’s wife is put first, before the house, whereas Exodus names the house first; Clarke and Ellicott note that “his field” is added here, not found in Exodus 20:17, and Ellicott explains the fitness of the addition — Israel had now become, or were just about to become, landowners in Canaan, so the coveting of a neighbour’s field is named where land was now their possession. They observe the slight change of word, “desire” being used of the wife and “covet” of the goods, marking the wife as not a mere possession but a person. They note Wesley’s plain sense of the command: thou shalt not desire anything that is not thine own, anything thou hast not; and why shouldst thou, seeing God hath given thee whatever truly tends to thy good? — contentment with God’s allotment is the cure of covetousness. They observe that the tenth commandment, reaching the heart, shows the spiritual nature of the whole law and the need of inward holiness. They note that it convicts every conscience. The verse thus gives the tenth commandment — the words then ending, as the next verse declares.
22:
On Deuteronomy 5:22 — “These words the LORD spake… with a great voice: and he added no more. And he wrote them in two tables of stone, and delivered them unto me” — the witnesses mark the Decalogue complete, written on two tables.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: “he added no more” — he spoke no more in this manner; or, there were only ten commandments. The Reformed Gill: the ten words spoken audibly by the Lord himself, heard by the whole congregation. Calvin: that there be no doubt of the law’s authority, the presence of God was manifested by sure tokens. Wesley: out of the fire, cloud, and thick darkness — a dispensation of terror, to make the gospel of grace more welcome; he added no more.

The verse closes the Decalogue. God spoke these ten words and no more, and wrote them on two tables. The law of the covenant is complete and engraved.

So the witnesses see the ten commandments closed and sealed: God spoke these words, and no more in that manner, and wrote them with his own hand on two tables of stone. The witnesses note the manner of the speaking: “with a great voice… out of the midst of the fire, of the cloud, and of the thick darkness” — Gill observes the ten words were spoken audibly by the Lord himself, heard by the whole congregation; Wesley marks that this was a dispensation of terror, the law given amid fire and darkness, designed (he says) to make the gospel of grace the more welcome, and to be a specimen of the terrors of the judgment-day (Hebrews 12:18). They mark the closing words: “he added no more” — Ellicott explains this to mean either that God spoke no more in that immediate and awful manner (the rest of the law coming through Moses), or that there were only ten commandments, the Decalogue complete in itself; the ten words are the whole foundation, to which nothing of that kind was added. They observe Calvin’s point that God manifested his presence by sure and dreadful tokens, that there might be no doubt of the law’s divine authority. They note the engraving: “he wrote them in two tables of stone, and delivered them unto me” — the commandments, spoken to the ear, were also written by God’s own hand on stone, that they might endure, the abiding charter of the covenant, committed to Moses. They observe the dignity the witnesses see in the Decalogue thus marked off: the ten words, spoken by God, complete and written in stone, stand as the foundation of the whole law. They note that what God writes endures. The verse thus closes the Decalogue — the people’s terror recalled in the next verses.
23:
On Deuteronomy 5:23 — “And it came to pass, when ye heard the voice out of the midst of the darkness… that ye came near unto me, even all the heads of your tribes, and your elders” — the witnesses mark the people’s terror at the voice.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke refers to Exodus 20:18. The Puritan Henry: the consternation the people were put into by the terror with which the law was given; they owned their desire of a mediator. The Reformed Calvin: lest they undervalue Moses’ teaching, he reminds them it was done at their own petition.

The verse recalls their fear. Hearing the voice from the darkness, the people drew near in dread. The terror of Sinai drives them to Moses.

So the witnesses see Moses recall the terror that seized the people when they heard God’s voice from the fire and darkness. The witnesses note the dread: “when ye heard the voice out of the midst of the darkness… ye came near unto me” — the people, overwhelmed by the awful voice and the burning mountain wrapped in thick darkness, drew near to Moses; the heads of the tribes and the elders came forward as representatives of all, unable to bear the immediate presence of God. They mark Henry’s observation of the consternation into which the giving of the law cast them: the terror was so great that they could not endure that God should speak more to them directly, and they owned their need of a mediator to stand between. They observe Calvin’s point in Moses’ recalling it: that he reminds the people of their own petition, lest afterward they should undervalue his teaching as merely a man’s — for the mediation of Moses was at their own request, born of their fear; they had themselves asked for one to stand between them and God. They note the lesson the witnesses draw: that the law, given amid such terror, showed the people their distance from the holy God and their need of a mediator; the very dread of Sinai prepared the way for the gospel and for Christ (Hebrews 12:18). They observe that sinful men cannot bear the unveiled presence of God, but need a go-between. They note that the people’s fear was just, and their request for a mediator right. The verse thus recalls their terror — their words to Moses following in the next verses.
24:
On Deuteronomy 5:24 — “And ye said, Behold, the LORD our God hath shewed us his glory and his greatness, and we have heard his voice out of the midst of the fire: we have seen this day that God doth talk with man, and he liveth” — the witnesses mark the people’s confession of God’s glory.

The Reformed Gill: the LORD showed them his glory and greatness in descending on Sinai and giving the law with such solemnity. Calvin: by their own confession they oblige themselves to obedience; confessing they saw God’s glory, they bind themselves to obey, unless they would convict themselves of ingratitude.

The verse gives their confession. They own God showed his glory, and yet they lived after hearing his voice. They confess the wonder of God speaking with man.

So the witnesses see the people confess the glory and greatness of God they had witnessed at Sinai, and marvel that they yet lived. The witnesses note their acknowledgment: “the LORD our God hath shewed us his glory and his greatness” — Gill observes they owned the glory and majesty God had displayed in descending on the mount and giving the law with such awful solemnity; the people confessed what they had seen and heard. They mark their wonder at surviving: “we have seen this day that God doth talk with man, and he liveth” — they marvelled that, having heard the very voice of God out of the fire, they were yet alive, for they had thought no man could see or hear God and live (Exodus 33:20); it was a wonder of mercy that they were spared. They observe the use Calvin makes of their confession: that by it they bound themselves to obedience; for, confessing that they had seen God’s glory and heard his voice, they laid upon themselves the necessity of obeying, unless they would convict themselves of ingratitude and unbelief; their own words were a pledge against them if they should afterward rebel. They note the lesson the witnesses draw: that those who have seen and confessed the greatness of God are the more bound to obey him; the confession of his glory carries the obligation of obedience. They observe that the people spoke truly, owning God’s majesty and their own preservation. They note that to know God’s greatness is to be bound to his will. The verse thus gives their confession — their fear of dying following in the next verse.
25:
On Deuteronomy 5:25 — “Now therefore why should we die? for this great fire will consume us: if we hear the voice of the LORD our God any more, then we shall die” — the witnesses mark the people’s fear of death.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: why should we die? for this great fire will consume us. The Reformed Gill: being now alive and escaped, let us be careful not to be exposed again. Wesley: we shall never be able to endure any farther discourse from God in such a terrible manner, but shall sink under the burden of it.

The verse gives their fear. They dread that to hear God again will be their death. The terror is more than they can bear.

So the witnesses see the people, having survived the voice, plead that they cannot bear to hear it again and live. The witnesses note their reasoning: “why should we die? for this great fire will consume us” — Gill observes that, being now alive and wonderfully escaped, they would not expose themselves again to the danger; they feared that the great fire of the mountain would consume them if the awful speaking continued. They mark their conviction: “if we hear the voice of the LORD our God any more, then we shall die” — Wesley draws out their sense, that though God had for this once kept them alive, they could never endure any farther discourse from him in so terrible a manner, but would surely sink and perish under the burden of it; the immediate voice of God was more than flesh could bear. They observe the truth the witnesses see in their fear: that sinful and mortal man cannot stand before the unveiled majesty and holiness of God; the fire that did not consume them once would, they felt, consume them if they came nearer; their dread was the just sense of the creature before the Creator and of the sinner before the Holy One. They note that this fear drove them to seek a mediator, as the next verses show; they could not come to God directly, but needed one to go between. They observe that the terror of the law thus prepared the way for the mercy of a go-between, and ultimately for Christ. They note that man’s frailty cannot endure God’s immediate presence. The verse thus gives their fear of death — pressed home in the next verse.
26:
On Deuteronomy 5:26 — “For who is there of all flesh, that hath heard the voice of the living God speaking out of the midst of the fire, as we have, and lived?” — the witnesses mark the wonder that they heard God and lived.

The Reformed Gill: what man in any age was ever heard of? Calvin: “flesh” is used in contempt, for the human race, when its frailty and perishing condition is meant. Wesley: “flesh” here is man in his frail, corruptible, and mortal state.

The verse marvels at the mercy. What mortal ever heard the living God and lived? They wonder at their preservation.

So the witnesses see the people marvel that they, frail mortals, had heard the living God speak from the fire and yet survived. The witnesses note the wonder: “who is there of all flesh, that hath heard the voice of the living God… as we have, and lived?” — Gill marks the force of it, that no man in any age had ever been known to hear God speak so directly and live; it was a thing without parallel, that a whole nation should hear the very voice of God and not be consumed. They mark the word “flesh,” which Calvin and Wesley note is used here with a sense of lowliness and frailty: “flesh” stands for man in his frail, corruptible, mortal state, set over against “the living God”; the contrast is between weak, dying man and the eternal, life-giving God, that such weakness should stand before such majesty and live was the wonder. They observe the truth the witnesses see: that it was pure mercy that preserved them; the holiness of the living God might justly have consumed frail flesh that drew so near, and only his forbearance spared them. They note the fitness of their reasoning: feeling their frailty before the living God, they rightly judged they could not bear to hear him again, and sought a mediator. They observe that the gulf between the living God and dying man is bridged only by God’s mercy and a go-between. They note that to hear God and live is itself a grace. The verse thus marvels at the mercy — their request to Moses following in the next verse.
27:
On Deuteronomy 5:27 — “Go thou near, and hear all that the LORD our God shall say… and we will hear it, and do it” — the witnesses mark the people’s request for a mediator.

The Reformed Gill: “go thou near” — to the mount, and to God on it; they ask Moses to draw near and receive God’s word for them.

The verse gives their request. They ask Moses to go near and hear for them, promising to obey. They seek a go-between and pledge obedience.

So the witnesses see the people ask Moses to be their mediator, to draw near to God and bring them his word. The witnesses note the request: “go thou near, and hear all that the LORD our God shall say” — Gill observes they ask Moses to go up to the mount, and to God upon it, and to receive the divine word in their stead; unable to bear God’s immediate voice, they would have Moses stand between and report to them what God spoke. They mark the promise they join to it: “and speak thou unto us all that the LORD our God shall speak unto thee; and we will hear it, and do it” — they pledge to hear and obey whatever God should say through Moses; the people undertake obedience, owning Moses as the appointed channel of God’s word. They observe the fitness of the request the witnesses see: that, feeling their frailty and fear, they rightly sought a mediator, and rightly bound themselves to obey God’s word so conveyed; the asking was good, and (the next verses show) God approved it. They note the typology the witnesses draw: that the people’s seeking of a mediator, one to go between them and the holy God and bring them his word, foreshadows the office of Christ, the one Mediator, through whom alone sinners draw near to God and receive his word (1 Timothy 2:5; Hebrews 12:24). They observe that the promise to obey, though sincere, would prove beyond their strength, as the lament of v. 29 implies. They note that to seek a mediator and pledge obedience was right. The verse thus gives their request — God’s answer following in the next verses.
28:
On Deuteronomy 5:28 — “And the LORD heard the voice of your words… and the LORD said unto me… they have well said all that they have spoken” — the witnesses mark God’s approval of the people’s request.

The Reformed Gill: the LORD heard the voice of their words, not only as he hears all that is spoken, but with approbation; he approved their request.

The verse gives God’s approval. The LORD heard and said they had well spoken. Their request for a mediator is owned by God.

So the witnesses see God hear and approve the people’s request for a mediator. The witnesses note the divine hearing: “the LORD heard the voice of your words” — Gill observes that God heard them, not merely in the general way in which he hears all that men speak, but with approbation; he gave ear to their request and owned it as good. They mark the approval expressed: “they have well said all that they have spoken” — God himself pronounced their words good; their fear of his immediate voice, their seeking of a mediator, and their promise to obey were all approved by him; the people had judged rightly of their own frailty and of their need. They observe the comfort the witnesses see in this: that God did not despise the people’s fear nor reject their request, but accepted it, and provided the mediation they sought; he met their weakness with condescension, speaking to them thenceforth through Moses. They note the deeper note that the next verse will sound: that, while approving their words, God yet knew the inconstancy of their hearts, and would soon wish for in them a heart to match their good profession. They observe that God owns what is right in his people’s desires, even while he reads the weakness behind them. They note that the request for a mediator was according to God’s own mind, who had ever purposed to come to his people through a go-between. They observe that God’s approval set his seal on the office of the mediator. The verse thus gives God’s approval — his lament over their hearts following in the next verse.
29:
On Deuteronomy 5:29 — “O that there were such an heart in them, that they would fear me, and keep all my commandments always, that it might be well with them, and with their children for ever!” — the witnesses mark God’s lament over the people’s heart.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: rather, “Who will give that they had such a heart?” — they refuse to receive such a heart from God; who then can supply it? The Reformed Gill: not that there are properly such wishes in God, but Scripture speaks after the manner of men. Calvin: God signifies they would not be so firm in keeping as ready to promise; hypocrisy was not purged from their minds. Wesley: the God of heaven is truly and earnestly desirous of the salvation of sinners.

The verse gives God’s wish. O that they had a heart to fear and obey him always! The lament reveals the people’s deep need.

So the witnesses see God, having approved the people’s words, lament that they lacked the heart to make them good. The witnesses note the yearning: “O that there were such an heart in them, that they would fear me, and keep all my commandments always” — God expresses, after the manner of men, a longing that the people might have a heart answerable to their fair profession; for, as Calvin observes, they would not prove so firm and faithful in keeping their promise as they were ready and willing to make it, and hypocrisy was not yet purged from their minds; their words were good, but their hearts unstable. They mark the differing ways the witnesses guard and read this divine “wish.” Gill is careful to say there are not properly such volitions or unfulfilled wishes in God, who works all his pleasure; the Scripture speaks after the language of men, partly to upbraid the people’s want of heart, partly to show what is pleasing to him. Clarke renders it as a question, “Who will give that they had such a heart?” and presses the point that the people refused to receive such a heart from God — and who then can supply it but God himself? Wesley takes it as a true and earnest token that God desires the salvation of sinners, and has given abundant proof that he is so. They observe the great truth the witnesses see beneath the words: that the people lacked, and could not of themselves produce, the new heart that the keeping of the law required; the law could command obedience but not create the heart to obey, and so the verse points beyond itself to the promise of a new heart under the gospel (Jeremiah 31:33; Ezekiel 36:26). They note that God’s lament both convicts man’s impotence and reveals his own gracious desire. The verse thus gives God’s wish — the people sent to their tents in the next verses.
30:
On Deuteronomy 5:30 — “Go say to them, Get you into your tents again” — the witnesses mark the people sent back to their tents.

The Reformed Gill: they had been brought by Moses, at God’s direction, to the foot of Mount Sinai to receive the law; this done, they are ordered to return to their tents. Calvin: God plainly subjoins his consent to the people’s prayer, ratifying it by his decree; whence it follows that to refuse to obey Moses is perverseness.

The verse sends them home. The people are bidden to return to their tents. The terror is over; the mediation begins.

So the witnesses see God dismiss the people to their tents, the immediate giving of the law being done. The witnesses note the command: “get you into your tents again” — Gill observes that the people had been brought by Moses, at God’s direction, to the foot of Mount Sinai to receive the law from God’s own mouth; that being now done, and their request for a mediator granted, they are ordered to return to their tents, their part at the mount being finished. They mark the significance Calvin draws out: that by sending them home and keeping Moses with him (next verse), God plainly subjoins his consent to the people’s prayer for a mediator, ratifying their request by his own decree; the dismissal is the seal of his approval. They observe what Calvin infers from this: that since God himself had thus appointed Moses as the channel of his word, for the people afterward to refuse to obey Moses would be not weakness but perverseness and rebellion against God’s own arrangement; in despising the mediator they would despise the God who gave him. They note the mercy the witnesses see: that God spared the people the terror of his immediate voice, sending them to the safety of their tents, and would thenceforth speak to them through Moses; he met their frailty with kindness. They observe that the people’s direct dealing with the awful presence was now over, and the way of mediation established. They note that to reject the appointed mediator is to reject God. The verse thus sends them home — Moses kept back in the next verse.
31:
On Deuteronomy 5:31 — “But as for thee, stand thou here by me, and I will speak unto thee all the commandments… which thou shalt teach them” — the witnesses mark Moses kept to receive the law.

The Reformed Gill: Moses was not permitted to go to his tent with the people, but ordered to stand by the Lord on the mount, to receive instructions from him.

The verse keeps Moses. He is to stand by God and receive the commandments to teach the people. The mediator remains to learn God’s word.

So the witnesses see Moses kept back, while the people are dismissed, to stand by God and receive the law for them. The witnesses note the distinction made: “but as for thee, stand thou here by me” — Gill observes that Moses, unlike the people, was not permitted to return to his tent, but was ordered to remain and stand by the Lord upon the mount, to wait upon him and receive his instructions; the mediator must abide in God’s presence to learn his will. They mark the charge given him: “I will speak unto thee all the commandments, and the statutes, and the judgments… which thou shalt teach them” — God would give Moses the whole body of the law, the further statutes and judgments beyond the ten words, that Moses might teach them to the people; the mediator was to receive from God and deliver to the people. They observe the office the witnesses see established: Moses standing between, hearing from God what the people could not bear to hear, and teaching them what he heard; the pattern of all true prophecy and ministry, to receive from God and deliver faithfully to men. They note the honour and the burden put on Moses: that he should stand alone in the presence of God, where the people dared not, and bear his word to them; a place of nearness and of trust. They observe the typology the witnesses carry: that Moses, kept in God’s presence to receive and teach the law, foreshadows Christ, who is in the bosom of the Father and has declared him (John 1:18). They note that the law was to be taught, not merely given. The verse thus keeps Moses to receive the law — the exhortation to obey following in the next verses.
32:
On Deuteronomy 5:32 — “Ye shall observe to do therefore as the LORD your God hath commanded you: ye shall not turn aside to the right hand or to the left” — the witnesses mark the charge to obey without swerving.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: he who marks not the word of God is never likely to fulfil his will; the way of truth and righteousness is a right line, and a man must walk straight on. The Reformed Gill and Calvin: observe every precept, as to matter and manner; be content with the law’s unadulterated teaching, receiving as right whatever God commands.

The verse charges obedience. They are to do as commanded, turning neither right nor left. The straight path of God’s law is to be kept.

So the witnesses see Moses charge the people to obey God’s commandments exactly, swerving neither to the one side nor the other. The witnesses note the charge: “ye shall observe to do therefore as the LORD your God hath commanded you” — Gill observes they were to observe every precept, both as to its matter and its manner, doing all that God commanded and in the way he commanded it, under a sense of the great obligation his giving of the law had laid on them. They mark the figure of the straight path: “ye shall not turn aside to the right hand or to the left” — Clarke draws it out, that the way of truth and righteousness is a right line, and a man must walk straight on in it, declining neither to excess on the one hand nor to defect on the other; to swerve either way is to leave the path. They observe Calvin’s point that the charge binds the people not only to embrace the law but to be content with its pure and unadulterated teaching, receiving as just and right whatever God has commanded, and neither adding to it nor taking from it; obedience is to the whole law, as God gave it, without the mixtures of men. They note the lesson the witnesses draw: that true obedience keeps the straight line of God’s word, neither turning aside to the right hand by superstition and will-worship, nor to the left by neglect and license; the path of duty is narrow and direct. They observe that he who marks not God’s word will never fulfil his will. They note that the whole law is to be kept, not a part chosen. The verse thus charges obedience without swerving — the promise of life and well-being following in the next verse.
33:
On Deuteronomy 5:33 — “Ye shall walk in all the ways which the LORD your God hath commanded you, that ye may live, and that it may be well with you, and that ye may prolong your days in the land” — the witnesses mark the promise of life in the way of obedience.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: God never gave a commandment he did not design man should obey; he who selects only the precepts he is inclined to keep deceives himself. The Reformed Gill: walk in all the ways God commanded, none to be avoided on any consideration; that ye may live, corporally and prosperously.

The verse promises life. Walk in all God’s ways, that it may be well and your days prolonged. Obedience is the way of life and blessing.

So the witnesses see the chapter close with the promise of life and well-being to those who walk in all God’s ways. The witnesses note the breadth of the obedience required: “ye shall walk in all the ways which the LORD your God hath commanded you” — Gill observes that none of God’s commanded ways were to be avoided or departed from on any consideration whatever; the people were to walk in the whole compass of his commandments, not in some chosen part. They mark Clarke’s pointed application: that God never gave a commandment he did not design man should obey; and he who selects from the divine testimonies only such precepts as he feels little inclination to transgress, and lives in the neglect of the rest, deceives himself; partial obedience is no obedience. They observe the threefold promise annexed: “that ye may live, and that it may be well with you, and that ye may prolong your days in the land” — life, well-being, and length of days in the promised land were the reward of obedience; the way of God’s commandments was the way of blessing and life. They note the principle the witnesses see, which the New Testament confirms: that the law promised life to those who kept it (Leviticus 18:5; Romans 10:5), though, as the gospel shows, none could so keep it as to earn the life it promised, and so it points to Christ, who kept it for his people. They observe that obedience and blessing are joined in God’s ordering; the way of duty is the way of true good. They note that the chapter ends, as it began, in the call to do all God commands. The verse thus promises life in the way of obedience — closing the discourse of the covenant and the law.
